summary: Gruber highlights two new third-party Mac apps that restore national flag icons to the input language switcher menu, after Apple removed them in MacOS 12.4. Apple replaced flags with two-letter country codes as part of a broader effort to decouple national flags from language selection, since flags carry political connotations that languages don't. While Gruber acknowledges Apple's reasoning makes sense, he notes the change was unpopular with polyglots who relied on colorful flag icons for quick visual scanning. He compares the two apps, giving a slight edge to Keyboard Switcheroo for its polish and additional customization options.
key_points: Apple removed national flag icons from the MacOS input menu in 12.4, replacing them with two-letter codes, as part of decoupling flags from languages
The policy change makes sense politically but hurts usability for multilingual users who relied on visual scanning of colorful icons
Two third-party developers quickly filled the gap with similar flag-restoration apps priced at $1-2
Keyboard Switcheroo offers more polish and customization, including flat flags, emoji flags, or custom images, plus in-app language editing
money_quotes: National flags carry political connotations that languages alone do not.
It's unfortunate for users accustomed to scanning the menu for colorful icons at a glance when switching.
Two third-party developers have come to the rescue, with similar apps that restore the 'pick a flag to change input sources' functionality.
Keyboard Switcheroo is a bit more polished.
key_insight: Apple's principled decision to decouple flags from languages in MacOS created a usability gap that third-party developers quickly filled, illustrating the healthy dynamic between platform decisions and the app ecosystem.
